summary: Score outcomes not process — remove coordination tool-call credit from composite scores, replace with git-diff-based work leverage metrics
context: Analysis of 9 benchmark runs (1,569 sessions) revealed scorer biases: shared-markdown's 15.5-point advantage over full-twining was largely caused by (1) pattern detection matching removed lines as positive evidence, (2) coordination file text inflating pattern counts, (3) process-compliance credits (15pts for calling twining_assemble) in composite scores. These biases masked the true signal.
rationale: The core research principle is: if two conditions produce identical code, they must score identically. Process measures (did the agent call a tool?) should be explanatory variables, not outcome scores. Work leverage (rework ratio, continuation index, line survival rate) measures the same coordination quality from git artifacts without referencing which tools were used.

alternatives.option: Keep process measures in composite with reduced weight
alternatives.reason_rejected: Any weight creates bias toward conditions that have coordination tools. A condition without tools can never earn those points regardless of code quality.

alternatives.option: Remove process measures entirely (don't track at all)
alternatives.reason_rejected: Process data is valuable for understanding WHY outcomes differ — just shouldn't inflate the score. Keep as explanatory metadata.
